learning_objectives: Define UX strategy as the intersection of business strategy, user needs, and technology
Map UX maturity levels in organizations and identify growth opportunities
Build a business case for UX investment using ROI frameworks
readings: UX Strategy by Jaime Levy, Ch. 1-3
Measuring the User Experience by Tullis & Albert, Ch. 12

lecture_notes.introduction: UX strategy connects design decisions to business outcomes. It ensures that UX work is not just aesthetically pleasing but strategically aligned with organizational goals and measurable in business terms.
key_concepts: UX strategy: aligning user experience with business objectives
UX maturity models: from unaware to user-driven organizations
ROI of UX: reduced development costs, increased conversions, lower support volume
Competitive UX analysis: evaluating competitor experiences systematically
North Star metrics: identifying the one metric that captures product-user alignment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a UX business case for a checkout redesign — calculating projected ROI from reduced abandonment, fewer support tickets, and increased conversion

review.value: ROI = (Gain from Investment - Cost of Investment) / Cost of Investment. For UX: gains include increased conversions, reduced support calls, lower development rework, and higher retention.
The single metric that best captures the value your product delivers to users — for Spotify it is time spent listening, for Airbnb it is nights booked. UX strategy should optimize toward this metric.

class_schedule.topic: Competitive UX Analysis and Market Positioning
learning_objectives: Conduct systematic competitive UX analyses using structured frameworks
Identify UX differentiation opportunities through competitor gap analysis
Position a product's UX strategy relative to market alternatives
readings: UX Strategy by Jaime Levy, Ch. 4-5
Blue Ocean Strategy by Kim & Mauborgne, Ch. 2 (strategy canvas)

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding the competitive landscape is essential for UX strategy. Competitive analysis reveals where your product can differentiate through experience rather than just features.
key_concepts: Competitive UX audit: evaluating competitors across key experience dimensions
Strategy canvas: visualizing feature/experience positioning across competitors
Blue ocean strategy: finding uncontested market space through UX innovation
Feature parity vs. experience differentiation
Competitive benchmarking: SUS scores, task success rates, NPS across competitors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a strategy canvas for three competing food delivery apps — plotting their UX strengths and identifying whitespace opportunities

review.value: A visual chart plotting competing products on key experience dimensions (ease of onboarding, search quality, checkout speed) — reveals where competitors cluster and where differentiation opportunities exist.
When features are commoditized, experience becomes the differentiator. Zoom won video conferencing not on features but on the simplest join experience — one click, no account required.

learning_objectives: Define UX-specific OKRs that connect to business objectives
Select and implement UX metrics that drive meaningful improvement
Avoid vanity metrics and common measurement pitfalls
readings: Measure What Matters by John Doerr, Ch. 1-3
Lean Analytics by Alistair Croll, Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: If UX cannot be measured, it cannot be strategically managed. UX metrics translate qualitative experience quality into quantitative signals that organizations can track, compare, and optimize.
key_concepts: UX OKR framework: Objectives (qualitative goals) + Key Results (measurable outcomes)
Leading vs. lagging indicators: task success rate (leading) predicts retention (lagging)
HEART framework: Happiness, Engagement, Adoption, Retention, Task success
Pirate metrics (AARRR): Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Revenue, Referral
Avoiding vanity metrics: page views and downloads do not measure experience quality
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting UX OKRs for a SaaS product — defining objectives, selecting key results using HEART framework, and creating a measurement dashboard

review.value: Google's UX metrics framework: Happiness (satisfaction, NPS), Engagement (frequency, depth), Adoption (new users, feature adoption), Retention (churn, return rate), Task success (completion, efficiency).

Task success rate is a leading indicator — it predicts future retention. Churn rate is a lagging indicator — by the time you see it, users have already left. Optimize leading indicators to improve lagging ones.

learning_objectives: Navigate organizational politics to advance UX priorities
Present design work to executives using business language
Build cross-functional alignment on UX strategy
readings: Articulating Design Decisions by Tom Greever, Ch. 1-5
Org Design for Design Orgs by Merholz & Skinner, Ch. 5-6

lecture_notes.introduction: UX strategy is only as good as its organizational support. Learning to communicate design value in business language, manage stakeholders, and build cross-functional alliances is essential for strategic impact.
key_concepts: Stakeholder mapping: influence, interest, and attitude toward UX
Translating UX outcomes into business language: revenue, cost, risk
Executive presentations: data first, solutions second, opinions last
Building UX champions in engineering, product, and executive teams
Design sprints as organizational alignment tools
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-playing an executive stakeholder presentation — pitching a UX initiative using business metrics, competitive data, and projected ROI

review.value: Executives do not care about 'better usability.' They care about: reduced support costs ($500K/year), increased conversion (3.2% to 4.1%), faster time-to-market (20% fewer design iterations). Translate UX into these terms.
Plot stakeholders by influence (ability to approve/block) and interest (engagement with UX). High-influence, low-interest stakeholders need proactive communication to prevent surprises.

class_schedule.topic: UX Strategy in Practice: Case Studies
learning_objectives: Analyze real-world UX strategy case studies for patterns and lessons
Develop a UX strategy roadmap for a product or organization
Present a UX strategy with clear rationale and expected outcomes
readings: UX Strategy by Jaime Levy, Ch. 8-9
Inspired by Marty Cagan, Ch. on Product Strategy

lecture_notes.introduction: UX strategy becomes real through roadmaps, prioritization, and sustained execution. This session synthesizes all strategic concepts into a practical planning framework.
key_concepts: UX strategy roadmap: now, next, later prioritization
Balancing quick wins with long-term strategic initiatives
UX debt: identifying and systematically reducing accumulated design problems
Aligning UX roadmap with product and engineering roadmaps
Strategy review cadence: quarterly reassessment based on metrics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a UX strategy roadmap for a real product — categorizing initiatives into now/next/later, estimating impact and effort, and aligning with business priorities

review.value: Accumulated design problems from shortcuts, outdated patterns, and inconsistencies — just like technical debt, UX debt compounds over time and must be systematically addressed or it degrades the entire experience.

Now: committed work for this quarter. Next: planned work for next quarter (scope may change). Later: strategic vision for 6+ months (direction, not details). This format communicates certainty levels honestly.

questions.question: A startup CEO asks 'Why should we invest in UX when we need to ship features fast?' Build a 3-point argument for UX investment that speaks in business terms.
questions.answer: Three business arguments: (1) Reduced development cost — every dollar spent on UX research before development saves $10-100 in rework. Finding and fixing a usability problem during design costs 1/10th of fixing it after launch. (2) Increased revenue — better checkout UX directly increases conversion rates. A 1% conversion improvement on $10M revenue equals $100K annually. Competitor analysis can quantify the opportunity. (3) Lower support costs — products with poor UX generate support tickets. Each support ticket costs $5-15 to resolve. Improving the top 5 usability issues can reduce support volume by 20-30%, saving thousands monthly. UX investment has measurable ROI — it is not an expense, it is a cost-avoidance and revenue-generation strategy.

rubric.key: Three distinct business arguments with specifics

questions.question: Explain the difference between a North Star metric and a vanity metric. Give one example of each for a social media platform.
questions.answer: A North Star metric captures the core value the product delivers to users and predicts long-term business success. For a social media platform: 'daily active users who post or meaningfully engage with content' — this measures genuine value exchange between users and the platform. A vanity metric looks impressive but does not indicate real value or predict business outcomes. For a social media platform: 'total registered accounts' — this number only goes up, includes inactive and spam accounts, and tells you nothing about whether users actually find value. The distinction: North Star metrics are actionable (teams can improve them through better UX), correlated with business outcomes, and reflect genuine user value. Vanity metrics feel good but do not drive decisions.

rubric.key: Correct definition of North Star metric
Valid examples for each with clear contrast

questions.question: Using Google's HEART framework, define one metric for each of the five dimensions for an e-commerce mobile app.
questions.answer: Happiness: Post-purchase satisfaction score (CSAT 1-5 after order completion). Engagement: Average browsing sessions per week per active user. Adoption: Percentage of users who complete their first purchase within 7 days of download. Retention: 30-day retention rate (users who return to the app within 30 days of first use). Task success: Checkout completion rate (users who start checkout vs. those who complete it). Each metric is specific, measurable, and directly related to the e-commerce context. Together they provide a holistic view of the mobile app UX.

rubric.key: All five HEART dimensions addressed with valid metrics
Metrics are specific and measurable, not vague
Metrics are appropriate for e-commerce context

questions.question: You need to present a UX redesign initiative to the VP of Engineering who is skeptical about UX value. How would you frame the presentation differently than you would for a design team?
questions.answer: For the VP of Engineering, reframe entirely in terms they value: (1) Lead with engineering impact data: 'The current design generates 40% of our support tickets. This redesign targets the top 5 usability issues, projected to reduce support-related engineering escalations by 25%.' (2) Show development efficiency: 'A design system component library will reduce front-end development time by 30% and eliminate the 15 hours/sprint currently spent on design-developer alignment.' (3) Use their metrics: reduced bug count, faster sprint velocity, lower technical debt from consistent components. (4) Present the timeline in sprints, not weeks. (5) Acknowledge trade-offs honestly: 'This requires 2 sprints of investment upfront. Here is the payoff curve showing break-even at sprint 4.' Never use design jargon. Speak in engineering terms: efficiency, scalability, maintainability, and measurable outcomes.

major_assignments.title: UX Strategy Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive UX strategy for a real product or organization. Include competitive analysis, UX metrics framework, strategic roadmap, and business case presentation.
objectives: Conduct competitive UX analysis and identify differentiation opportunities
Define UX OKRs using the HEART framework
Build a prioritized UX roadmap aligned with business objectives
Present the strategy in business terms with projected ROI
deliverables: Competitive UX analysis with strategy canvas for 3+ competitors
UX OKR document with HEART metrics and measurement plan
UX strategy roadmap (now/next/later) with impact estimates
Business case presentation (10 minutes) with ROI projections

criteria: 3+ competitors systematically evaluated on UX dimensions
Strategy canvas reveals clear differentiation opportunities
Analysis is evidence-based, not opinion-based
Insights are actionable for strategy formulation
HEART framework applied with specific, measurable metrics
Leading and lagging indicators identified
Measurement plan is realistic and implementable
Metrics connect to business outcomes
Initiatives are prioritized by impact and effort
Now/next/later provides appropriate certainty levels
Roadmap aligns with business and product priorities
Quick wins balanced with strategic investments
Presented in business language with quantified projections
ROI calculation is credible and well-supported
Stakeholder concerns addressed proactively
Presentation is compelling and professional
Evidence-based competitive analysis with clear differentiation opportunities
HEART metrics framework with measurable, business-connected indicators
Prioritized roadmap aligning UX with business objectives
Compelling business case with credible ROI projections

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on UX strategy frameworks, metrics, and business communication

strategy_exercises.description: Weekly exercises on competitive analysis, metrics definition, and stakeholder communication

ux_strategy_plan.description: Comprehensive UX strategy with competitive analysis, metrics, roadmap, and business case

case_study_analyses.description: Written analyses of real-world UX strategy case studies

final_presentation.description: Executive-style presentation of UX strategy to industry professionals

course_policies.attendance: Class participation is essential as sessions involve strategic discussions and role-playing exercises. More than 3 absences affect participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Strategy deliverables build sequentially. Late work penalized 10% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Competitive analysis research may be shared. All strategy documents and presentations must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Business data and metrics must be based on real research or clearly marked as estimates with stated assumptions. Fabricating competitive data is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Spreadsheet software for ROI calculations, presentation software, Figma for strategy canvas visualizations, and access to competitor products for analysis.
